input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-07T15:55:25.491Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.

failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference — Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated this as a success.

Let me look at their project files to understand the setup.

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>

    <!-- Treat discovering zero tests as success (exit code 0 instead of 8). -->
    <TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>false</TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>

<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>

    <!-- Treat discovering zero tests as success (exit code 0 instead of 8). -->
    <TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>false</TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>

data.reasoningText:
Hmm, it still returns exit code 8. Let me check if the MSBuild property name is correct. Maybe it needs to be a different property name, or maybe it needs to be passed as a command-line argument instead.

Let me research the correct property. The property might be `--ignore-exit-code` or something like that. Let me check what options are available.

Actually, looking at the Microsoft.Testing.Platform documentation:
- Exit code 8 means "no tests were found"
- The property to control this behavior might be different

Let me check the available options.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Usage TestProject [option providers] [extension option providers]

Execute a .NET Test Application.

Options:
    --config-file
        Specifies a testconfig.json file.

    --diagnostic
        Enable the diagnostic logging. The default log level is 'Trace'.
        The file will be written in the output directory with the name log_[yyMMddHHmmssfff].diag

    --diagnostic-filelogger-synchronouswrite
        Force the built-in file logger to write the log synchronously.
        Useful for scenario where you don't want to lose any log (i.e. in case of crash).
        Note that this is slowing down the test execution.

    --diagnostic-output-directory
        Output directory of the diagnostic logging.
        If not specified the file will be generated inside the default 'TestResults' directory.

    --diagnostic-output-fileprefix
        Prefix for the log file name that will replace '[log]_.'

    --diagnostic-verbosity
        Define the level of the verbosity for the --diagnostic.
        The available values are 'Trace', 'Debug', 'Information', 'Warning', 'Error', and 'Critical'.

    --exit-on-process-exit
        Exit the test process if dependent process exits. PID must be provided.

    --help
        Show the command line help.

    --ignore-exit-code
        Do not report non successful exit value for specific exit codes
        (e.g. '--ignore-exit-code 8;9' ignore exit code 8 and 9 and will return 0 in these case)

    --info
        Display .NET test application information.

    --list-tests
        List available tests.

    --minimum-expected-tests
        Specifies the minimum number of tests that are expected to run.

    --results-directory
        The directory where the test results are going to be placed.
        If the specified directory doesn't exist, it's created.
        The default is TestResults in the directory that contains the test application.

    --timeout
        A global test execution timeout.
        Takes one argument as string in the format <value>[h|m|s] where 'value' is float.


Extension options:
    --coverage
        Collect the code coverage using dotnet-coverage tool

    --coverage-output
        Output file

    --coverage-output-format
        Output file format. Supported values: 'coverage', 'xml' and 'cobertura'

    --coverage-settings
        XML code coverage settings

    --filter
        Filters tests using the given expression. For more information, see the Filter option details section. For more information and examples on how to use selective unit test filtering, see https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/testing/selective-unit-tests.

    --maximum-failed-tests
        Specifies a maximum number of test failures that, when exceeded, will abort the test run.

    --no-ansi
        Disable outputting ANSI escape characters to screen.

    --no-progress
<exited with exit code 0>

data.reasoningText:
I see the `--ignore-exit-code` option. But a better approach might be to use the `--minimum-expected-tests 0` option. However, the MSBuild property approach would be more permanent. Let me check if there's an MSBuild property for `--ignore-exit-code` or `--minimum-expected-tests`.

Actually, the cleanest fix is to use the MSBuild property to set `--ignore-exit-code 8`. Let me look at what MSBuild properties map to these CLI flags.

Looking at the help output:
- `--ignore-exit-code`: "Do not report non successful exit value for specific exit codes (e.g. 
'--ignore-exit-code 8;9' ignore exit code 8 and 9 and will return 0 in these case)"
- `--minimum-expected-tests`: "Specifies the minimum number of tests that are expected to run."

The `--minimum-expected-tests 0` would set the minimum to 0, which should allow zero tests without failing. But actually, the default minimum is likely 0 already... exit code 8 might be specifically about "no tests found" which is different.

Let me try the `--ignore-exit-code 8` approach. But really, I want to set this as an MSBuild property so it's always applied.

I need to find the right MSBuild property name—it could be `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` or something similar. Let me revert my last change and test `--minimum-expected-tests 0` to see if that handles the zero-tests case properly.

data.content: That works. `dotnet test` picks up the MSBuild property and returns exit code 0. Let me verify the final file:
data.reasoningText:
It works via `dotnet test`. The `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` MSBuild property is picked up by `dotnet test` but not when running the test assembly directly via `dotnet run`.

This is the expected CI workflow — people use `dotnet test`, not `dotnet run`. Let me verify the final csproj looks good.

data.content:
Here's what's happening and the fix:

**Problem:** Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns **exit code 8** when zero tests are discovered — unlike VSTest which returned 0. This is by design but breaks CI for assemblies that conditionally compile out all tests.

**Fix:** Add this MSBuild property to your `.csproj`:

```xml
<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
```

This tells the test platform to treat exit code 8 (no tests found) as success (exit code 0). Actual test failures (exit code 2) are still reported correctly.

If you have many test projects, you can put this in a `Directory.Build.props` instead so it applies everywhere.
